subject: Chemistry
title: The Exxon Valdez Oil Spill
description: One-minute skill: The Exxon Valdez Oil Spill.

hook.text: In 1989, the Exxon Valdez tanker hit a reef in Alaska, spilling 41 million liters of crude oil into pristine waters.

buildup.text: Crude oil is a complex mix of hydrocarbons. In cold water, it spreads, sinks, and coats everything it touches.

discovery.text: The spill killed 250,000 seabirds, 2,800 sea otters, and 300 harbor seals. Oil persists in the sediment to this day.

twist.text: Hot-water cleaning actually made things worse—it killed the microbes that naturally break down oil.

climax.text: The spill led to the Oil Pollution Act of 1990, requiring double-hull tankers and better response plans.

punchline.text: One reef, one hull breach, 2,000 km of coastline contaminated. Cold oil doesn't evaporate.

quiz.question: Why was hot-water cleaning counterproductive after the Exxon Valdez spill?
options: It killed the natural microbes that break down oil in the environment
Hot water caused the oil to sink deeper into sediment
The heated oil released more toxic fumes into the air
